data.title: TSPY: 14% And Growing Income To Fund Your Retirement
data.description:
In November, last year, I identified the TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF (TSPY), which is a covered call ETF vehicle focused on high current income generation from the selling of the S&P 500 (SPY) implied volatility.

From that moment, after carrying out a detailed assessment, TSPY has become my Top 1 pick in the overall S&P 500-linked covered call space.

If we take a step back and ask ourselves what the available opportunity set is for us to go long on the S&P 500 index while enjoying the fruit of harvested volatility premiums (i.e., high dividends).

We will arrive at the following alternatives:

  • 0DTE (Zero Days to Expiration) vehicle that sells options on the index level - e.g., the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE).
  • At-the-money (or ATM) vehicle that sells options on the index level - e.g., the Global X S&P 500® Covered Call ETF (XYLD).
  • Out-of-the-money (or OTM) vehicle that sells options on the index level - e.g., the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI).
  • OTM vehicle that sells options on the index level but adds an active stock selection component - e.g., the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI).

The lower we move down the list, the smaller the dividend yields become, but so does the prospect for the ETF to capture price-driven upside of the S&P 500.

For example, since JEPI sells calls on a meaningful OTM basis in order to minimize the opportunity cost, it offers only ~7.9% of dividend income, which in my eyes is simply too low for a covered call ETF instrument.

Given that my portfolio exposure that I am willing to dedicate to covered call ETFs is not unlimited, I want to maximize the benefit that comes with such products - i.e., enhancing the overall portfolio yield.

For example, if I included JEPI in the mix, I would not get any marginal increase to my current income streams considering that such high-quality gems (my portfolio core) as Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) and Altria Group, Inc. (MO) provide comparable if not higher yields.

Conversely, going all-in on the juiciest yield instrument like XDTE (yielding ~36%), which is an aggressive (in a positive sense) 0DTE covered call vehicle, seems suboptimal in the current market environment. The biggest risk that I see is the value-erosive sequence of returns, e.g., a sharp drop that is followed by a significant recovery in the subsequent day. In this case, the risk is high that XDTE locks in a 0DTE contract that is based on a depressed S&P 500 strike value (after day 1) and because of that fails to participate in an upside recovery process (day 2).

In my view, TSPY offers a golden middle route, which has become even more enticing than when I published my original thesis. Let me explain.

Thesis Review

In the chart below, I have compared how all of these S&P 500 covered call ETF alternatives have performed over the past 12-month period:

What is clearly evident is that the occurrence of more meaningful drawdowns can indeed introduce a drag to ATM and pure-play 0DTE products — XYLD and XDTE, respectively. Another interesting observation is that JEPI has failed to earn alpha-return points through its active stock selection process, which of course might reverse itself as we go forward from here.

Another interesting observation is that TSPY and SPYI have exhibited almost identical correlation even though TSPY has shaped its strategy around 0DTE options, while SPYI has assumed a dynamically managed OTM approach, just like some other best-of-breed funds — e.g., the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) and the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX).

The only difference between these two in the registered return context is that TSPY has put (and still does) heavier emphasis on yield, while SPYI has left bigger room for price return realization.

And exactly this is why, currently 14.1% yielding, TSPY suits my philosophy as an income-focused investor slightly better than SPYI.

Now, having said that, the TSPY investment case has, in my humble opinion, strengthened over the past couple of months. But before I elaborate on that, let me quickly flesh out the reason/driver that has made TSPY so correlated to SPYI.

The reason is quite simple. TSPY is also dynamically managed, which opens the door for the Management to make selected adjustments in terms of opportunity cost minimization or option premium maximization (this depends on market circumstances). However, the biggest driver is the fact that TSPY does not sell ATM or very aggressive 0DTE option contracts. Instead, it has defined a 0% to 5% out-of-moneyness range within which the Management can write calls. The closer the sold 0DTE contract strikes move to the 5% bound, the lower the yields, but so does the opportunity cost (risk), making the vehicle effectively more similar to an OTM fund like SPYI.

Since the Management has been more inclined towards the opportunity cost avoidance and there have not been bigger one-day SPY jumps than ~5%, it is only logical that TSPY has performed in a similar fashion to SPYI.

Speaking of TSPY return prospects, I have to once again say that my level of bullishness (conviction) has significantly increased. Here is why.

The implied volatility, which is the primary driver for option premium and thus income for covered call ETFs, has been elevated for quite some time. Currently, the implied vol is 17%, while the historical average is 12%.

More importantly, the overall market environment bodes well for having a higher-for-longer VIX (implied vol) scenario in place. It is very difficult to project how long we will be able to enjoy the benefit of heightened VIX, but my base assumption is that as long as the equity valuations remain close to record high levels, we should be able to (at minimum) periodically experience stronger turbulence in the market, which, in turn, should feed into richer option premiums (and thus enhanced attractiveness of dividends).

For example, we have the dollar weakening trade, rotation out from growth into value (bad for the S&P 500), inflation uncertainty, inconclusive job market data points, increased geopolitical tensions, etc. Meanwhile, the equity valuations are still close to all-time highs, which provide a great ground for swift repricing whenever a marginal data point emerges that has a theoretically negative impact on the growth stock return prospects.

The repricing to the downside will, of course, negatively impact TSPY just as the S&P 500 itself because the collected 0DTE option premiums in any single day do not constitute a meaningful amount of capital that would translate into really improved downside protection. Here is a great, recent example (where we can also see the beauty of TSPY's strategy to leave part of the upside always open as opposed to what pure-play 0DTE covered call ETFs do):

At the same time, the recent volatility of the S&P 500 index stemming from the SaaS fears has proved yet again that higher vol does not imply massive selloffs. Instead, it just makes the overall ride bumpier, which is what translates to higher VIX, but not deep and prolonged drawdowns. The lack of huge declines reduces the risk of significant one-day recoveries that would exceed the ~5% OTM bound and inflict damage to TSPY.

All of this is extremely supportive for high and even growing monthly distributions. We can also see how this has been playing out on the ground. Namely, since the VIX stepped into a higher level territory (from October 2025), TSPY has been able to grow its monthly current cash flows by ~3% or slightly above 6% on an annualized basis.

The Bottom Line

In a nutshell, TSPY is a covered call ETF, which strikes a balance between yield and option income maximization through 0DTEs and the opportunity cost, which might materialize in the case of a more pronounced increase of the S&P 500 index.

The elevated volatility that we have now and the market environment that we have in front of us both support consistent and even growing distributions for TSPY, which in the context of already offered ~14.1% yield makes the offer truly attractive for income investors.

Of course, we have to be cognizant of the risk that is associated with a major drawdown of the S&P 500, which, if not followed by a relatively quick recovery, might very likely lead to TSPY's current income decrease. As elaborated above, TSPY is able to recapture lost NAV to the extent that any recovery that happens at the SPY level does not exceed 5% (or, in reality, we should assume a lower ceiling as the management might not necessarily always operate close to the maximum policy of 5% out of moneyness). An S&P 500 recovery that, in a single day, exceeds the 0DTE option strike is a pure foregone return, or the so-called opportunity cost.

There is no mitigant to this risk unless (except for the collected option income, which should provide a slight edge or offset to the downside) we gravitate away from the S&P 500 or the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) indices by moving our capital into covered call ETFs that are based on different underlyings, such as gold (GLD), with the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI), and MLPs (AMLP), with the NEOS MLP & Energy Infrastructure High Income ETF (MLPI).

Having said that, I think that it is still important that we as income investors retain some form of growth technology exposure in our portfolios that, otherwise, without covered call ETFs like TSPY, would be heavily underrepresented due to suboptimal yield offerings (oftentimes no dividends at all).

data.title: Alphabet Is Doing Well Under CEO Sundar Pichai
data.description:
Introduction

Per my December article, Alphabet Inc., aka Google (GOOG, GOOGL), changed from a Buy to a Hold at the time when the GOOGL share price was $315.81. We now have the Q4 '25 numbers, and Google did a nice job closing out the year.

My thesis is that Google is doing well under CEO Sundar Pichai.

The Numbers

An August 2015 8-K filing from Alphabet announced structural changes, including Sundar Pichai becoming the new CEO of Google. Technically, Google is part of Alphabet, but almost all of Alphabet’s economics come from Google, so CEO Pichai has been in control for a little more than a decade. In a December 2019 8-K filing, co-founders Larry Page and Sergey Brin chose to transition from their roles as CEO and President, respectively, of Alphabet. As such, Google CEO Pichai became CEO of Alphabet as well. In his entire tenure at the helm of Google, CEO Pichai has been focused on AI, and shareholders have been rewarded as a result.

Coming from the Chrome browser team, CEO Pichai has a deep understanding of the importance of AI with respect to digital advertising in search and YouTube. Once promoted to CEO, it didn’t take him long to talk about AI and ML. In the Q4 '25 call, he talked about ML making products like Gmail better:

Machine learning is making our products smarter and more useful for people every day. It powers Smart Reply, in Inbox by Gmail, which suggests short responses that are relevant to an incoming email. People absolutely love this feature. In fact, in just a few months, Smart Reply already makes up 10% of all mobile responses in Inbox.

In April 2016, he wrote about moving from a mobile-first to an AI-first world (emphasis added):

Looking to the future, the next big step will be for the very concept of the “device” to fade away. Over time, the computer itself - whatever its form factor - will be an intelligent assistant helping you through your day. We will move from mobile first to an AI first world.

Under the leadership of CEO Pichai, Google’s revenue has climbed from $75 billion in 2015 to $402.8 billion in 2025 for a 10-year CAGR of more than 18%. Here is a visual of this remarkable growth:

YouTube

CEO Pichai was given good cards when he took over in 2015, but it is important to recognize him playing them well. Other leaders given the same opportunities would have mediocre results by now instead of prodigious results. One of the good cards is YouTube, which Google bought for just $1.65 billion in 2006. YouTube had $40.4 billion in ad revenue in 2025 plus about $20 billion in subscription revenue to bring the annual total to more than $60 billion!

YouTube is an asset that clearly benefits from AI as it democratizes content. Given the AI video creation tools of today and tomorrow, we no longer have a content bottleneck with multi-million dollar studios. YouTube allows pretty much anyone to put content up for virtually no cost, and the rev-share agreement allows creators to grow incrementally without having to take enormous risks. CEO Pichai must have seen this coming a long time ago, and it is enjoyable to see this content distribution tool changing the world.

Search And Gemini

The best card is the search advertising business - and CEO Pichai knows it well, seeing as he came from the Chrome team. CEO Pichai and Apple (AAPL) CEO Tim Cook have been prescient in terms of creating win-win scenarios for the two companies. A May 2025 court filing reveals the way Google and Apple work together to generate enormous amounts of money. They have been discussing a Gemini App distribution agreement to power Apple intelligence since at least 2024. In January 2026, Google and Apple made a joint statement about Apple foundation models being based on Gemini. The court filing goes on to talk about how Google knows Chrome is a key distribution mechanism for AI tools like Gemini, and it quotes Google as saying Chrome will

continue to evolve from being a ‘window to the web’ into a personalized, helpful user agent that can adapt to each person’s unique, multifaceted needs.

Google’s 2021 search revenue was exceptionally high as people stayed home during the covid pandemic. Once restrictions from covid started changing, co-founder Sergey Brin started going back to the office a little bit. He then ran into an OpenAI (OPENAI) employee at a party and had a conversation about AI and computer science. He mentions this conversation and the impact it had on him in a May 2025 podcast. This made him decide to unretire and come into the office more often. He worked with CEO Pichai to make sure some of the bureaucracy was taken away, and an example of a silly rule he had to change was that Gemini was on the “no list” for what employees could use for coding.

In late 2022, the threat of ChatGPT made everyone question the future of search. The NY Times talks about how CEO Pichai issued a code red to address the threat. Like co-founder Brin, CEO Pichai made improvements to address the bureaucracy, expediting more than 20 AI initiatives.

Google Cloud

CEO Pichai made a big bet on the cloud many years ago, and he had to be patient as the business developed. From Q4 '19 to Q4 '22, Google Cloud had cumulative operating losses of $12.9 billion. Not many companies are capable of this type of commitment, but now it is paying off. In Q4 '25, Google Cloud had operating income of $5.3 billion on revenue of $17.7 billion which was up dramatically from operating income of just $2.1 billion on revenue of $12 billion in Q4 '24.

Having both Nvidia (NVDA) and custom TPU hardware, Google Cloud offers what many believe are the two best choices for running AI. A rack-scale system is one in which the entire rack, which is composed of dozens of GPUs, acts as one giant computer. Right now, Nvidia, Google TPUs, and Amazon Trainium are the only options for rack-scale architecture. This is one reason why these three silicon options will continue to grow nicely and continue pulling ahead of others. Along with Tesla (TSLA) designing chips themselves, these three AI hardware options are the only ones Tesla CEO Elon Musk mentions in his February interview with Dwarkesh Patel about having data centers in space. Google Cloud’s annual revenue run rate is already over $70 billion, and given the way it is growing, I won’t be surprised if the annual run rate is $100 billion by Q4 '26.

The Future

CEO Pichai talks about the future with Andrew Ross Sorkin in the last few minutes of the December 2024 DealBook Summit. He says he deeply cares about seeing the journey through on AI after setting the company to be AI-first in 2015. He thinks the decade ahead is going to be the most interesting in this respect. Being AI-first is not just about doing well with LLMs. He reminds us Google has the full stack, and it is the same piece of technology that improves YouTube, Cloud, and Waymo. He is optimistic about Google doing well as a company in the decade ahead.

Valuation

Future free cash flow (“FCF”) on a per-share basis is what matters most from a valuation standpoint. Back in 2015, FCF was $16.1 billion, seeing as we had OCF of $26 billion less capex of $9.9 billion. There was also $5.2 billion in stock-based compensation, and there are strong arguments this should be treated as a cash expense, but it is partially offset by growth capex. The $16.1 billion FCF was 83% of the $19.4 billion operating income and almost 22% of the $75 billion revenue.

By 2025, FCF dropped to a lower percentage of operating income and revenue as management told us we have substantial amounts of growth capex. In 2025, we had FCF of $73.3 billion, which came from $164.7 billion of OCF less $91.4 billion of capex. There was also $25 billion of stock-based compensation, which impacts our per-share thinking. The $73.3 billion FCF was just 57% of the $129 billion operating income and a mere 18% of the $402.8 billion revenue.

One of the big valuation risks is if growth capex investments don’t pan out. Management expects capex to increase by about 100%, or $90 billion, from $91.4 billion in 2025 to a range of $175 to $185 billion for 2026. OCF isn’t expected to increase by anything close to this (after starting 2024 at $125.3 billion, a 31.4% jump brought the 2025 OCF to $164.7 billion).

This all points to a year-over-year decline for Google’s 2026 FCF, though it should avoid the negative territory expected elsewhere. A primary example of this pressure is the $200 billion 2026 capex estimate relative to the 2025 OCF of $139.5 billion at Amazon (AMZN).

It can be easier to visualize these FCF and capex concerns in a spreadsheet where we look at the three hyperscalers along with Meta (META) ($ in billions):

The capex investments they’re making can bring about great rewards, but they can also bring about severe issues if the depreciation schedules prove to be too optimistic. I value the services segment at 20 to 25x trailing twelve-month (“TTM”) operating income, but first I subtract the Alphabet-level activities operating income hit, which I think mostly applies to services. This means we have $139.4 billion in services operating income less $16.8 billion in operating losses from Alphabet-level activities for a figure of $122.6 billion. Applying a multiple of 20 to 25x gives us a range of $2,450 to $3,065 billion when rounded to the nearest $5 billion.

I think Google Cloud is worth 25 to 30x the annualized operating income of $21.2 billion ($5.3 billion*4), which gives us a valuation range of $530 to $635 billion when rounding to the nearest $5 billion.

Waymo first offered paid rides in Phoenix before adding the Bay Area and then Los Angeles. Austin and Atlanta were added through Uber (UBER) in 2025 before Miami was added in January 2026. Between this and quantum physics plus some other considerations, I think the other bets segment is worth $150 to $200 billion.

The 2025 balance sheet shows an accounting figure of $68.7 billion for non-marketable securities, but the economic figure is higher. Per a January article from the NY Times, Anthropic is in talks for $10 billion in funding at a $350 billion valuation. Citing court documents, the article says Google owns about 14% of Anthropic, which puts their interest at almost $50 billion. Per a December 2025 Business Insider article, Google owns about 7% of SpaceX (SPACE), which should be worth a little over $100 billion when they IPO at around $1.5 trillion. This combination comes to $150 billion but it may be overstated because of dilution. Partially offsetting dilution concerns, we can make an estimate for less notable names in this line - maybe $10 billion.

Here is my sum of the parts valuation:

$2,450 to $3,065 billion services

$530 to $635 billion cloud

$150 to $200 billion other bets

$150 to $160 billion non-marketable securities

-------------------------------------

$3,280 to $4,060 billion total

Per the 2025 10-K, we have 5,822 million A shares plus 837 million B shares as of January 28, 2026, for a subtotal of 6,659 million shares, which we multiply by the February 17 GOOGL share price of $302.02 to get a subtotal of $2,011 billion. Additionally, we have 5,438 million C shares, which we multiply by the February 17 GOOG share price of $302.82 to get another subtotal of $1,647 billion. Adding these together, the market cap is $3.66 trillion.

Again, at the time of my December article, the GOOGL price was higher at $315.81, and I thought it was a hold, as the excellent Q4 '25 numbers hadn’t come in yet and the price was above my valuation range. The market cap is now inside my valuation range, and I think the stock is between a hold and a buy.

Long-term investors who do not already have Google as a full position should look for future volatility to provide buying opportunities. ChatGPT took off in late 2022, so GOOGL was under $100 per share most of the time from late September 2022 to early March 2023. Obviously this was a great time to buy the stock. In early 2025, GOOGL was briefly under $150 per share, and it climbed to over $300 by the end of the year. Clearly Google didn’t become twice as valuable during the year, as it was well underpriced at the $150 level. More buying opportunities should present themselves sooner or later, and forward-looking investors should keep an eye out for them.

data.title: EXI: Industrials Dashboard For February
data.description:
This monthly article series offers a top-down analysis of the GICS Industrials sector based on value, quality, and momentum metrics. It may also help to analyze sector ETFs such as the Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics. This article focuses on an alternative with international diversification: iShares Global Industrials ETF (EXI).

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), and Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on the trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY, and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, and GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for transportation in the table below is the 11-year average of the median earnings yield in transportation companies. The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by subsector (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

The most notable change is an improvement of value and quality scores in the aerospace/defense subsector. Valuation has also improved in services/distribution and deteriorated in building/equipment.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum scores based on median returns.

Interpretation

Based on my latest S&P 500 dashboard, industrials are the second most overpriced GICS sector. Nonetheless, all subsectors are not equal: transportation is only marginally overvalued relative to 11-year averages, while other industries are overvalued by 21% to 35%. data.title: Tesla: The Most Volatile Stock That's Worth Holding Onto
data.description:
Since Elon Musk took over as CEO in 2008, Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has been a company that forces investors to choose between short-term discomfort and long-term conviction.

At $417.07 per share, with a market capitalization of approximately $1.42 trillion, today is truly no different; Tesla is not being valued as a car company any longer. It is being valued as a platform company, one that is building everything from autonomy and robotics to energy infrastructure and AI capabilities all at once.

That’s why, despite the current automotive slowdown, this is not a time to panic-sell Tesla stock. It is a time to Hold and wait for change.

First, let’s start with the objective facts.

Tesla’s Q4 results confirmed what the market had already sensed, the decline and global competition of the car business. Total automotive revenue has declined 11% annually. Which reflects the end of certain government tax incentives as well as heightened competitive pressure in China and Europe.

Deliveries of Teslas were down sequentially, and operating income fell by 11% all while operating expenses surged 39%, which then compressed the operating margin to 5.7%. Tariffs alone impacted the quarter by more than $500 million!

But here’s the thing: while automotive revenue declined, Tesla’s energy generation and storage segment grew 25%! The company achieved record Megapack deployments and reported nearly $12.8 billion in annual energy revenue, which represented a growth of 26.6%.

So what does this mean for Tesla now?

It means energy is no longer a side project for them. It’s now becoming a structural foundation in the stock.

The demand for grid-scale storage has continued to remain structurally strong as well. Tesla has mentioned plans for further expansion with Megapack 3 and Megablock. However, Tesla has acknowledged that energy margins may face compression due to low-cost competition, tariffs, and policy uncertainty.

The growth trajectory for energy, however, ultimately helps diversify Tesla’s revenue base. Especially during this time of automotive slowdown. So while Tesla’s car business seems to be on the decline, Tesla itself is evolving into something new.

Before I give my own views on Tesla’s stock, let’s take a look at the numbers.

Now, while the one-month total return being -7.0% (vs. the S&P 500’s (SP500) -2.0%) and the three-month total return being -3.1% (vs. the S&P 500’s -0.2%), points toward weak short-term momentum.

It’s important to remember that short-term price action doesn’t define long-term positioning. Tesla has always been a high-beta stock; when volatility increases, Tesla typically exaggerates both upside and downside moves.

Over the last twelve months, Tesla has outperformed the broader market. Despite recent pullbacks, Tesla’s twelve-month total return is 24.0% (vs. the S&P 500’s 13.0%). The three-year average total return sits at 28.46% (vs. the S&P 500’s 22.4%), representing Tesla’s ability to outperform the index on an annualized basis despite extreme volatility.

While Tesla’s five-year average total return of 8.94% (vs. the S&P 500’s 14.8%) is more reflective of boom-bust volatility, it is not structural stagnation. As that number includes Tesla’s massive 2020 run-up as well as its severe 2022 drawdown. But one big reason I’m saying to hold involves looking at the last ten years.

The ten-year average total return for Tesla is 45.13% (vs. the S&P 500’s 26.7%). These last ten years include Near-bankruptcy fears, the exponential rally of 2020, the crash of 2022, rebound in 2023-24, as well as the ‘production hell’ that was 2017-19.

Despite the massive drawdowns, long-term holders have been rewarded disproportionately. Based on Tesla’s history, selling during volatility has often been the wrong move.

Elon Musk has recently updated Tesla’s mission to “amazing abundance,” and he emphasized that 2026 will be a major capital expenditure year for Tesla. The company expects CapEx to exceed $20 billion. They also plan to fund six factories and expand their AI compute infrastructure, battery manufacturing, solar cell production, and robotics development. This is aggressive reinvestment!

Historically, Tesla has had its most uncomfortable periods right before its most major shifts. The current volatility tells me that the company is clearly in another reinvestment cycle. Tesla is advancing Full Self-Driving, or FSD, technology, and Musk stated that the company has already conducted paid rides in Austin with no safety monitor in the vehicle.

FSD adoption has already reached nearly 1.1 million paid customers all around the world, with roughly 70% opting for upfront purchases. But the most important part of this is that Tesla is transitioning fully to a subscription-based FSD model! Which is something that can absolutely reshape revenue! A recurring software stream rather than a one-time vehicle sale?

If autonomy scales nationally, Tesla’s earnings model changes dramatically! After all, subscription revenue typically commands higher valuation multiples than hardware margins!

But if we’re talking about change, what is perhaps the boldest move that Tesla announced was the plan to wind down Model S and Model X production, as Tesla plans to convert the Fremont space into an Optimus production facility. Along with a long-term goal of manufacturing a million humanoid robots annually. That’s right, a million robots a year!

Rather than protecting legacy luxury models with relatively low volume, Tesla has instead decided to reallocate factory space for robotics!

It’s clear that Elon Musk has a vision of where he sees Tesla going in the future, and it’s not automotive. Tesla is signaling clearly that it views humanoid robotics as a future mass-market product category.

Now, investors must decide whether or not they believe in Elon Musk and Tesla’s vision.

Either way, a long period of volatility is sure to come. But, whether you like him or not, one thing has remained consistent over the past two decades, and that’s that the market has repeatedly underestimated Elon Musk’s long-term execution.

When legacy automakers dismissed electric vehicles as niche products with limited range and weak consumer appeal, what did Elon Musk do? He pushed Tesla to scale EV production globally.

Not only that, he also had Tesla vertically integrate its battery technology, power electronics, and software stack in ways traditional manufacturers avoided. Tesla then started to build its own battery architecture, designed custom AI chips, and in the end, reduced its dependence on outside supply! Those were strategic decisions that, while quite expensive upfront, led to being structurally advantageous over time for Tesla.

He also constructed a global charging infrastructure when others were too scared to do so, understanding early on that range anxiety would be one of the greatest adoption barriers to EVs. The Supercharger network was seen as extremely aggressive, both strategically and financially, at the time, but it became a competitive moat long before competitors recognized its importance. While also becoming one of Tesla’s strongest competitive advantages

Elon Musk saw a vision of Tesla’s future at that time, when it was still a small company with limited production volume and inconsistent profitability. Tesla is not being managed for the next quarter’s earnings print. It’s being managed for platform dominance over the next decade. That approach inevitably introduces short-term turbulence, but it also creates the potential for long-duration competitive advantages.

In my five-year valuation, the next year's EPS estimate is $2.62, and the forward P/E ratio is 159.19. The estimated five-year annual growth is 12%, and the PEG ratio is 13.27, which calculates to a five-year target price of $689.10 and a five-year total return potential of 65.22%, and the value grade in my ‘Best Stocks Now’ app is currently C-. As always, there are a lot of wild cards up Musk’s sleeve, however.

If you’re selling Tesla today, that means you believe that autonomy will stall and robotics will fail to grow. It means you believe energy growth will fade away and that Tesla’s spending won’t generate long-term returns sufficient enough for you to hold.

Holding Tesla stock requires belief in execution, more specifically, belief in Elon Musk’s execution. In his ability to turn aggressive reinvestment into future-defining outcomes.

Because as things are right now, the automotive business is weak, competition is intense, and margins are compressed. However, energy is growing. Autonomy is entering a time of paid deployment, and robotics production is being prepared, all while AI infrastructure is being built!

As a money manager with 25 years of experience under my belt, I see the prudent move at this stage not to be aggressive accumulation of Tesla stock, nor do I see it to be reactive liquidation.

The best thing you can do right now is Hold on and let the investments either validate the valuation or prove the market wrong.

data.title: 5 Signs Of The Coming Correction
data.description:
Equity trading has gotten quite choppy here in February after stocks advanced in January to kick off January. Growing worries around the coming disruption from improving AI capabilities have played havoc with the SaaS concerns, insurance brokers, wealth management firms, commercial real estate services, and even trucking/logistical names over the past couple of weeks.

The NASDAQ sold off just over two percent last week but remains near all-time highs. Of note, the S&P VIX Index (VIX) has risen nearly 40% in 2026 as equity volatility has had a notable increase. There are also other potential signs a correction for the market is on the near-term horizon. In today's article, I discuss five things I am watching right now in this regard.

#1 - Insiders Are Picking Up Selling

Corporate insiders might have anticipated the choppiness we have seen in the markets so far in February. Roughly 1,000 corporate insiders sold stock in January, compared to just 207 that purchased equity to start 2026. This is the highest insider selling/buying ratio in five years.

#2 - Absurd Market Valuations:

I have noted many times in recent quarters that equity valuations are at levels not seen since the end of the Internet Boom. By some measures, stocks are in uncharted territory. One of these metrics is the so-called 'Buffett Indicator,' which has gotten to absurd levels viewed from a historical lens.

During the pandemic, the Federal Reserve boosted the money supply by some 40% over two years. This huge increase in money supply was a primary driver in price appreciation across many asset classes, including residential real estate and equities.

The MSCI USA Index's market cap relative to the US M2 money supply has risen to 270%. That is up 120% since 2022 and 40% higher than its peak reached just before the Internet Bust commenced.

#3 - Job Growth Is Anemic

Last week, the BLS disclosed job revisions that took total net jobs created in 2025 down to just 181,000. To be fair, the BLS radically took their original job projections in 2024 down sharply in 2025 as well. That means job growth last year averaged roughly 15,000 jobs per month. Outside of recessions, this is the weakest job growth in the U.S. since 2003.

In addition, job openings have fallen to their lowest levels in five years. And rapidly improving AI capabilities will make entire job categories much more vulnerable to significant layoffs in the quarters and years ahead. This is hardly going to be a positive for consumer sentiment, which is already in the toilet viewed from a historical lens. And consumer activity drives roughly two-thirds of U.S. GDP.

#4 - Market Concentration

The top 10 largest stocks by market capitalization now make up approximately 40% of the entire U.S. equity market. This is significantly higher than the peak during the Internet Boom and far higher than the height of the Nifty Fifty stage in the early 1970s. And as can be seen above, whenever there has been a surge in market concentration, the corrections that followed have been significant.

#5 - Volatility Is Surging Under The Surface

I presented how the VIX has risen nearly by half so far in 2026 in the opening narrative of this article. However, VIX is just one measure of volatility, albeit one of the most well-known.

Other metrics better show how volatility is surging right now. As noted earlier, fears of AI disruption have smacked several sectors in the market hard on a rotational basis over the past couple of weeks. The spread between average single-stock 2-week volatility and the Nasdaq 100's volatility is up to 20.7 points. This metric shows how much individual stocks are moving compared to the index. This spread has now doubled over the last month. This is three times higher than the 4-year average and is at the level since the Covid pandemic in 2020. As the saying goes, never short a dull market, and the market right now is anything but dull.

Other than the roughly one percent of my portfolio I have in long-dated, way-of-the-money bear put spreads I have on the major indexes, I don't short equities. That said, I do remain conservatively positioned, as the likelihood of a correction over the next six months seems to be increasing rapidly. Three-quarters of my portfolio is within covered call holdings around the few stocks I am finding in this overbought market that sport reasonable valuations. I have quite a few positions in the energy sector, including names like Devon Energy (DVN) and the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE). Interestingly, the Energy sector is the best-performing part of the market so far in 2026. The last time that could be said was in 2022, which was a lousy year for the overall market; it should be noted.

The rest of my portfolio is in short-term treasuries and cash awaiting lower entry points to slowly be deployed into the market if a correction materializes, as it feels increasingly likely in the months ahead.

data.title: Itron: Staying Bullish On Q4 Beat And Strategic M&A
data.description:
I've continued to rate Itron, Inc. (ITRI) as "Buy."

My bullishness on the name is supported by better-than-anticipated 4Q2025 EPS and value-enhancing inorganic initiatives.

I'm Pleased With Above-Consensus Q4 Earnings

The group issued an 8-K filing revealing its latest financials on Wednesday, Feb 18.

Its bottom line surged 82.2% year-on-year to $2.46/share for Oct-Dec '25. This represented a big reversal from the 16.3% decline in 3Q2025.

My September 15, 2025, piece highlighted that the firm was suffering from "delayed backlog realization" due to the "ongoing tariff war", as opposed to "permanent sales loss." I was proven right.

Its turnover contraction narrowed from 3Q25's -4.2% to 4Q25's -1.8% on sequential terms.

Management highlighted at the analyst briefing that "fourth quarter revenue of $572 million was higher than the range we expected." "Some of the delays that we saw in the middle of last year" were "exogenous events" that "have played through" according to its explanation.

ITRI's quarterly EPS also beat the sell-side's projection by 12.5%.

The company's revenue was still down 6.7% YoY, despite the QoQ improvement outlined earlier. But a shift in business composition enhanced its profitability.

ITRI touched on a better "customer and product mix" in its results slides. This was the factor driving up its normalized EBIT-to-sales by 4.4ppts (percentage points) to 15.9% for 4Q2025.

The enterprise's 4Q call commentary indicated that topline was "lower than the prior year due to planned portfolio changes." I think it made the right trade-off by giving up certain low-margin solutions and clients.

In my opinion, ITRI should do well going forward.

Its "ending backlog of $4.5 billion" disclosed in the earnings presentation is equivalent to almost twice its FY25 sales.

It was shared at the results meeting that the "pipeline" was "+27% from the end of '24 to the end of '25." Management's remarks suggested that a similar rate was observed year-to-date.

Separately, ITRI's guide points to a "mid-teens to 20%" growth in its "Annual Recurring Revenue/ARR" in FY2026.

ARR accounted for 15.5% of FY25 group-level topline. This is a predictable income source, which should enhance future financial visibility.

I forecast that ITRI will achieve a positive low-single-digit revenue increase again this year, versus 2025's 3% drop. I'm confident because of its 4Q25 outperformance and solid leading indicators.

Inorganic Expansion Moves Caught My Attention

I wrote in the mid-Sep '25 article that ITRI is likely to use "its robust cash flow generation to fund value-accretive acquisitions." That's precisely what happened.

M&A Deals

It allocated $0.85 billion of capital to the Locusview/Urbint investments in the past few months as detailed above.

Newly-Created Division Called "Resiliency Solutions/RS"

My take is that the RS business unit comprising the recently acquired Urbint/Locusview will boost ITRI's prospects in many ways.

Its FY25 company-wide "Gross Profit Margin/GPM" amounted to 38%. RS's estimated FY2026 GPM is +32ppts superior.

You would expect ITRI to become more profitable over time with the additional financial contribution from a higher-margin segment.

Also, Locusview and Urbint have untapped potential, which can be realized by their larger parent.

The new investees' client numbers are in the teens. ITRI's customer base is 8,000 strong. It's easy for me to imagine the huge revenue upside that can be derived from cross-selling.

I see that RS is well-positioned to benefit from structural trends.

"Global X ETFs's" analysis found that "extreme weather caused nearly half of the (1H2025 U.S. power) outages."

Urbint's "emergency preparedness and response" offering witnessed "3.5 million hours of restoration usage" during "Winter Storm Fern" based on ITRI's earnings call disclosures. That's a clear illustration of its value proposition.

It was also mentioned at the 4Q briefing that Locusview's "digital construction management" offering helps to "enhance the speed and integrity of grid build-out." These services should be in demand, considering that seven-tenths of America's transmission lines are over a quarter of a century old.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Risks

ITRI's future financial results could disappoint investors if portfolio mix optimization is executed poorly. The resulting top line contraction may more than offset the wider margins.

It's natural to be concerned that the M&A integration process doesn't proceed smoothly. In that scenario, the anticipated synergies pertaining to Locusview/Urbint might not be realized in full.

The Final Word

My view of ITRI is still favorable.

I'm sticking to the "Price-to-Earnings Growth/PEG" valuation methodology, which was employed in the Sep 15 update.

The stock's forward one fiscal year P/E is 16x. The consensus's FY2026-2028 earnings/share CAGR projection is +24%. I obtained these figures from S&P Capital IQ.

ITRI's shares can possibly appreciate by +50% (24/16) benchmarked against 1xPEG.

I've identified re-rating catalysts like a growing share of revenues generated by the high-GPM RS segment and an FY26 top line turnaround.

data.title: Rithm Capital: Common Stock Very Interesting, Preferred Stocks Are A Strong Buy
data.description:
Rithm Capital

Rithm Capital (RITM) is a mortgage REIT (mREIT). In the past it had primarily been invested in mortgages and MSRs (mortgage servicing rights). Mortgages tend to appreciate in value as mortgage interest rates fall, while MSRs appreciate in value when interest rates rise. A factor in the valuation of MSRs is refinancing risk. If a loan that RITM is servicing gets refinanced, they lose the servicing fees on that loan. When rates rise, there is little incentive for anyone to refinance their mortgage. But when interest rates fall, refinancings can increase. So RITM's MSRs act as a hedge for their mortgage portfolio. Currently the mortgages that RITM services have yields of about 4%. Thus, with mortgage rates much higher than that, the refinancing concern is not a large risk at the current time.

RITM has been managed very well. RITM has been clearly the best-performing mREIT over time and currently offers a nice 9.45% dividend yield. Here is a price chart for the last 5 years.

RITM is the dark blue line. As you can see from the chart, the last 5 years have been tough for mREITs. The mREIT ETF, ticker MORT, is down 38%, while RITM was up 7%. This is quite an impressive showing for RITM. Supposed blue chip, NLY, is down 33% during this time, and AGNC is down close to 30%. The closest peer to RITM is PMT, which is down about 35%. ARR is down 70% during this period.

The strange thing to me is that underperforming AGNC now trades at a massive premium of 40% to its book value, while RITM trades at a 16% discount to book value despite RITM being a much better performer than AGNC. If both traded at book value now, RITM would have been up over 20% in price over the last 5 years, while AGNC would be down over 50%. And if AGNC hadn't been able to sell a massive amount of shares at well above book value, they would be down even more than that. RITM just reported their book value at $12.66 per share. This makes RITM look like quite a bargain at $10.57 relative to some of its peers.

One of the things that RITM does is originate mortgages, which many mREITs do not do. Additionally, RITM has been making moves to diversify into new businesses, mostly expanding their asset management business. They have purchased Sculptor Capital, which manages private equity and hedge funds. They have also purchased Crestline Management, which is a private credit company. They have also bought Genesis Group, which owns high-end New York City office properties, and RITM also has investments in single-family residences. So it is becoming quite diversified and looking more like Blackstone, an asset manager who is also heavily into real estate.

RITM to Transform Into a C-Corp

With RITM in transition into other businesses, RITM expects to change its structure from a REIT to a C-corp. In the very recent conference call, management stated that "at some point we do need to be a C-corp". For simplicity sake, to be a REIT, 75% of a company's assets must be real estate related (mortgages and property owning, for example). At the rate that RITM is diversifying, the company will likely have no choice but to convert to a C corporation.

The likely consequences of this are a common stock dividend cut. They will no longer be forced to pay out 90% of their earnings as a REIT. That could rattle the stock, as mREIT investors are often yield chasers and value a company based on its current yield. This is certainly a poor investing strategy to buy common stocks based on their dividend rather than the value of their business. Current yield is simply an arbitrary number, and that dividend can be greater than the company's earnings, and it can be slashed at any time, crashing the stock. No doubt some yield chasers have experienced a crash when a dividend is cut.

A drop in price due to a dividend cut would be a buying opportunity for RITM. First of all, the dividend will now be "qualified" for a lower tax rate, but looking at RITM as a business, or as a value investor would, makes it very undervalued. RITM's cost of capital should drop as a result of becoming a C-corp, which will help earnings. RITM hopes that conversion to a more "asset management oriented" C-corp would eventually cause its price to rise significantly because asset managers like Blackstone (BX) trade at a high PE ratio of around 20, while RITM is currently trading with a PE of 5. If RITM simply traded at a PE of 11, that would double the stock price.

So RITM common stock could ultimately be a nice winner (high dividends plus big capital gains). But we don't quite know yet when this C-corp conversion will occur, so it may be early to be buying a large position now, especially with a dividend cut likely possibly causing a knee-jerk selloff at first. But long term, RITM is a strong buy, in my opinion, and I have a starter position in RITM's common shares. And it is rare for me to buy a common stock. On a conversion announcement, and especially if the stock sells off on a dividend cut, I will significantly add to my position.

RITM's Balance Sheet and Earnings

According to Yahoo Finance, analysts expect $2.31 in earnings this year. This makes for a PE of 5, making RITM look very cheap at the current price, especially for a company that is now partly an asset manager. As they move toward an asset-managing C-corp, this should bring in strong buying by asset-manager investors, replacing the fleeing high-yield dividend investors.

RITM tends to keep its leverage lower than other mREITs. Liabilities plus preferred stock is about 6 times common equity. AGNC operates at a leverage of 10.5 times common equity, although that can partly be justified by the fact that it invests only in agency mortgages. But there are mREITs like MITT, which are not agency mREITs, that have extremely high leverage of 15 times. They invest in non-qualifying loans and also home equity loans. This looks like a disaster waiting to happen for the yield chasers and makes RITM look extremely safe in comparison.

One negative for RITM common stockholders is that RITM has issued a large amount of common stock to keep leverage low given all of the acquisitions that they have made recently. The number of shares went from 520 million to 555 million this past year. With the common stock trading below book value, issuing common shares dilutes common shareholders, but if they are making good accretive acquisitions, this could be worth it.

But issuing common shares, regardless of whether they sell above or below book value, in order to keep their leverage low, is great for the safety of their preferred stocks. So let's talk about them.

RITM Preferred Stocks

RITM management's excellent relative performance in the mREIT sector combined with their low leverage and desire to maintain low leverage makes RITM preferred stocks look like solid mREIT preferred stocks from a credit risk perspective. And they have preferred stocks with great yields as well.

Taxation of RITM Preferred Stocks

Very importantly, the likelihood that their preferred stocks will become "qualified" dividend payers in the future will give them huge after-tax yields. Unlike many authors and services that ignore after-tax yield in their articles, at our service we are sharply focused on after-tax yields when making recommendations, as that is all that really matters.

And while RITM is still an mREIT, RITM common and preferred stocks also get a tax break on their dividends. That break is that 20% of its dividends are tax-free for U.S. investors.

RITM's Very Attractive Preferred Stocks

RITM-B (RITM.PR.B)

RITM-B is a floating-rate preferred stock. It floats at a yield equal to SOFR + .26% plus 5.64%. At its current price of $25.25, that gets you a current yield of 9.50%. With the Fed seemingly on hold, this is a very attractive yield. Even 2 or 3 quarter-point cuts by the Fed shouldn't be a big deal, as the yield will still look very strong relative to the new lower interest rate environment. And becoming a qualified dividend payer will offset a drop in yield because it will raise the after-tax yield.

Because RITM-B can be called, it trades at a 9.50% current yield versus fellow floater RITM-C, which has no call risk currently, and so it has a lower yield of 9.05%. If RITM-B had no call risk, it would trade at $26.45 for a 9.05% yield. So RITM-B is discounted by $1.20 per share due to call risk. But the call risk is virtually nonexistent. By "call risk" I mean losing money if RITM-B should be called. With it trading near par, accruing dividends rapidly due to its high yield, and the fact that RITM must give 30-day notice before calling RITM-B, there is really no call risk. Here are some examples of preferred stocks callable at any time with big call risk: GSL-B trading at $27.02, CMRE-D trading at $28.18, NLY-F trading at $25.99, and C-N trading over $29.

And a call is very unlikely anyway. RITM made a partial call of higher-yielding RITM-A some time back, but there are still 6 million shares of RITM-A outstanding. Those would be called before the shares of RITM-B would be called. Additionally, RITM has issued 2 preferred stocks recently and did not use the money raised to call any RITM-A. So it seems they are not looking to refinance RITM-A. So if they are not interested in calling more RITM-A, then calling RITM-B is extremely unlikely. In fact, one of our strategies at our service is to buy securities with call risk in order to get much higher yields and where the call risk looks quite minimal. RITM-B easily meets our criteria, even if it was trading at a higher price. I believe we have a very good price currently.

And in addition, we get a 20% tax break on RITM-B. So the after-tax yield on RITM-B is what you would get on a 10.06% to 10.68% yielding bond, depending on your tax bracket.

But when RITM becomes a C-corp paying qualified dividends, the after-tax yield now becomes the equivalent of a bond yielding from 10.65% to 12.15%. That huge 12.15% yield is for the upper 3 tax brackets. The 10.65% is for the 24% tax bracket.

But I think once that RITM converts to a C-corp, they may want to call RITM-B. But in the meantime, we are getting a very strong tax-benefitted yield.

RITM-A (RITM.PR.A)

RITM-A is very similar to RITM-B, but its yield is slightly higher. The reset formula for RITM-A is SOFR + .26% plus 5.802%. Its current yield at its current price of $25.49 is 9.55%. So you get a very slightly higher yield from RITM-A versus RITM-B, 9.55% versus 9.50%, but you take on more call risk because RITM-A will be called before RITM-B, and it trades at a larger premium to par, so there is more price downside on a call. Currently, I hold a large position in RITM-B and hold no RITM-A.

RITM-C (RITM.PR.C)

The floating rate drops off on RITM-C. It is SOFR + .26% plus 4.97%. At a price of $24.54, the current yield is 9.05%, a good amount lower than RITM-A and RITM-B. It is callable any time, but given that it is trading below par, a call at $25 would be quite a good thing.

But there are 2 ways that RITM-C could work out better than RITM-A and RITM-B. 1) is that RITM converts to a C-corp and calls the higher-yielding RITM-A and RITM-B but leaves RITM-C alone. RITM-C, with a 9.05% yield that is now qualified, will become very attractive and likely trade higher, giving us a higher return on RITM-C than RITM-A or RITM-B. 2) RITM-C is called along with RITM-A and RITM-B when they convert to a C corp. If that were to happen in, say, 8 months, RITM-C's yield to call would be 11.60%, which would generate a much higher return than you would get with RITM-A or RITM-B over the same period.

But of course there are more unknowns here. I expect they will convert to a C-corp, but that is not guaranteed, nor do we know when this might happen. I am thinking it will happen this year and maybe even by mid-year, but it could drag on longer than I expect. And we also don't know whether RITM-C will be called or not after the conversion, nor can we be certain that RITM-A and RITM-B will be called. But recently, with the probability of becoming a C corp, I have added a position in RITM-C but of smaller size than my positions in RITM-B, RITM-D, RITM-E, and RITM-F.

RITM-D (RITM.PR.D)

RITM-D is a reset-rate preferred stock and a fantastic shorter-term play. With its huge reset rate of the 5-year U.S. T-note yield + 6.223%, if not called in November of this year, its yield will reset to 10.10% at its current $24.79 price. While that would be wonderful, I have to believe that RITM-D will be called in November. Since RITM just issued a new reset-rate preferred stock with an 8.75% yield, I can't imagine they would want to pay 10% on RITM-D. They can easily refinance this preferred stock at a lower yield and lower reset-rate formula.

So we need to focus on RITM-D's yield-to-call (YTC). At its current price of $24.79, the YTC is 8.10%. That is a huge yield for a 9-month security. Certainly beats a 9-month CD. Let's compare that to baby bonds maturing in 2026.

RITM-D 8.10%

GAINN 5.60%

OCCIO 5.30%

OCCIN 7.20%

SWKHL 6.10% YTC - virtually certain to be called in 2026

SSSSL 6.30%

EICA 6.30%

So if you want a relatively short-term security, RITM-D is the best on the market. If you are building a bond ladder, like we do at our Conservative Income Portfolio service, RITM-D belongs there, and it is in our bond ladder. Sometimes you have to think a little outside of the box when you have a preferred stock that is more like a short-term bond and not miss out on RITM-D in your bond ladder for 2026.

And remember. The bonds in the above list are taxed at your top tax rate, while RITM-D's much higher yield also gets a 20% tax discount (20% of dividends are tax-free).

RITM-E (RITM.PR.E)

RITM-E is a fixed-rate preferred stock. Until recently, mREITs have been issuing fixed-to-floating preferred stocks, but not long ago, AGNC and NLY both issued a fixed-rate preferred stock, and now we have RITM-E.

At its current price of $24.73, RITM-E has an 8.90% yield. mREIT ARR has a fixed-rate preferred stock, ARR-C, but its current yield is only 8.20% versus 8.90% for RITM-E. And in the above price chart, we saw that over the last 5 years, ARR has fallen 70% in price, while RITM was actually up in price. So I am much more comfortable with RITM management than that of ARR.

Fixed-rate AGNCZ preferred stock has the same coupon as RITM-E, but AGNCZ currently sells well over par at $26.09 and has a YTC of 7.80%. YTC on RITM-E is 9.05%. Fixed-rate NLY-J is selling for $26.27. So fixed-rate mREIT preferred stocks AGNCZ and NLY-J would seem to have little price upside given their large premium to par. RITM-E, on the other hand, has room to move higher in price along with its higher yield.

The best thing about RITM-E is that it has close to 5 years of call protection, and in the likely event that RITM becomes a C corp, you will have locked in a very high qualified dividend for some years. The other RITM preferred stocks that I discussed above, especially RITM-A and also RITM-B, could well be called as a result of a change to a C corp.

Like the other RITM preferred stocks, their 8.90% current yield also gets you a 20% tax break until the conversion, and a future 8.90% "qualified" dividend will get you the after-tax yield that is equivalent to an 11.40% bond if you are in one of the top 3 brackets. And added to that very large after-tax return, I expect RITM-E to move higher in price, especially after a conversion of RITM to a "qualified dividend"-paying company and RITM-E's dividends become "qualified". The qualified dividend will really make a big difference in the attractiveness and fair value of RITM-E. So I expect it to move up in price, providing a strong total return from RITM-E.

RITM-F (RITM.PR.F)

RITM-F was recently IPO'd. It has the same coupon as RITM-E and 5-year call protection, which again is great, especially with a conversion to a "qualified" dividend. So the thesis for RITM-F is similar to RITM-E.

The primary difference between RITM-E and RITM-F is that if RITM-F is not called on its call date, the dividend will be reset to a yield that equals the 5-year T-note yield at that time plus 5.09%.

At a price of $25.04, the current yield on RITM-F is 8.80%. So you are paying a bit more for RITM-F than RITM-E and getting a bit of a lower yield, but you do have some protection against higher interest rates due to the resetting nature of RITM-F. But that protection does not kick in for 5 years. Additionally, if rates on the 5-year note fall over the next 5 years, RITM-F will see a decline in its dividend when it resets, whereas the RITM-E dividend is permanently fixed. But lower rates in 5 years could result in a call of RITM-E but not RITM-F. But with a "tax-benefited" 9.05% YTC, and probably qualified at some point, I don't worry about a call on RITM-E 5 years from now.

I love reset-rate preferred stocks and have recommended a number of them, but one negative is that at reset time, if the reset rate is very attractive, they can call the preferred stock from you. If, on the other hand, the reset rate is not particularly attractive, they can simply reset the dividend and not call it. Both RITM-E and RITM-F offer strong after-tax yields. At current prices, I have a slight preference for RITM-E, but I do own both.

Summary/Conclusion

Rithm Capital's management has been great, and RITM has massively outperformed the other mREITs in the sector over the last 5 years. RITM is the only mREIT that I know of that had a strong total return over the last 5 years. Given that it currently trades at a forward PE of 5, at a 16% NAV discount, and with a 9.45% yield, I rate RITM a long-term buy.

RITM has been rapidly expanding its asset management business. Thus, management has said that they need to convert to a C corp from a REIT. We just don't know exactly when they will do it, but if their non-real estate assets rise above 25%, they can no longer be an REIT. Asset managers trade at much higher PE ratios than RITM and at large premiums to book value. If RITM simply traded to an 11 or 12 PE, the price would double, and it would still be cheap on a book value and PE basis relative to similar asset managers like Blackstone.

Since we always focus on after-tax yield, converting to a C-corp will be great for their preferred stocks. This will make their already large dividends "qualified" dividends. This will give a big boost to the after-tax yields of their preferred stocks for U.S. investors.

For a high current yield, my favorite is RITM-B. Its current yield is 9.50%, and 20% of those dividends are tax-free for U.S. investors. So it is already a strong buy. If it isn't called when RITM becomes a C corp, this will become the best preferred stock in the market. But even if called, the high tax-benefitted yield you will get from now until it is called will be very generous.

If you want a shorter-term security, something that will almost certainly be redeemed this year, RITM-D is the best short-term security in the market. RITM-D is a reset-rate preferred stock with a call date of November 15th of this year. If not called, its dividend will reset to a 10.10% yield at its current price. And that will be either with a 20% tax break on the dividends or a "qualified" 10.10% yield. That is probably too good to be true, so I am assuming it will be called in November.

The yield to call is 8.10%, which is far better than any safe baby bond that matures in 2026. Typical 2026 baby bonds have YTMs around 6.3% with no tax benefits. So the tax-benefitted 8.10% yield-to-redemption of RITM-D is far and away the best short-term security.

RITM-E and RITM-F are the best securities if you want to get a very good return for the long term. RITM-F has 5-year call protection, and RITM-E has close to that. Thus, when these already high yielders convert to "qualified" dividend payers, they can't be called by RITM.

RITM-E is a fixed-rate preferred stock that currently yields 8.90% and sells below par. You won't find any reasonably safe 8.90% "qualified" dividend-paying preferred stocks in the market. When the conversion occurs, that should drive the price of RITM-E higher, especially with the multi-year call protection for a big after-tax total return. If you should decide to just hold it until its call date on November 15, 2030, the YTC is a tax-benefitted 9.05%.

RITM-F is fairly similar, except that in 5 years the yield can be reset if not called. It would be reset to the yield on the 5-year treasury note plus 5.09%. At the current 5-year note yield, the yield would be pretty much unchanged at 8.80%.

RITM-C is also a floating-rate preferred stock like RITM-A and RITM-B, but it trades at a lower current yield of 9.05% and trades below par. Although we don't know for sure what action RITM will take in terms of calling their floating rate preferreds when they convert to a C-corp, if all 3 are called, RITM-C is likely to provide the highest total return of the three. If RITM-C is called in 8 months, the yield-to-call will be a fat 11.60%.

And if not called, and it becomes a 9.05% "qualified" dividend payer, I would expect the price of RITM-C to rise. Although the likelihood of a C corp conversion is very high, it is not guaranteed. If that doesn't happen, then RITM-B will be a better place to be.

To sum up, with all of the RITM preferred stocks, their high yields, and 20% tax break from an excellent company, they are all strong buys here. The coming conversion to a C corp is just a big cherry on top for those preferred stocks that are not called. RITM-E and RITM-F, with their long call protection, would definitely be big beneficiaries of having their dividends become "qualified" dividend payers. But each preferred stock has its virtues, depending on what you are looking for.

data.title: Fortune Brands: Recovery Potential Amid A Soft Cycle
data.description:
Synopsis

Fortune Brands Innovations (FBIN) quarterly results were mixed because of the subdued housing environment and margin pressures. While in my previous coverage, I gave it a hold rating due to a lack of margins of safety; the valuation now appears more compelling. Despite modest growth, FBIN continues to demonstrate operating stability and market share gains. Coupled with earnings recovery potential, the improved risk-reward profile supports a Buy stance.

Fourth Quarter Earnings Analysis

FBIN’s results were mixed in Q4 2025. Sales totaled $1.1 billion, down by 2% year-over-year, while sales excluding China remained flat. Operating income for the quarter came in at $158 million, down by 13% year-over-year, with operating margins contracting to 14.7%. The weaker performance is primarily as a result of softer volumes and an unfavorable product mix. Management saw significant pressure as customers took a cautious stance on inventory build. Despite softer demand conditions and margin pressure, management noted continued market outperformance, supported by resilience in higher-end offerings and growth in connected products such as Flow and Yale.

Challenging Macro Environment

Recent US housing trends largely support the firm’s cautious view. While homeowners are still spending on repairs and renovations, activity levels remain steady rather than showing strong demand. Leading Indicator of Remodeling Activity [LIRA] is a good metric to track repair and remodel spending. It is projected that homeowner renovation and repair spending will grow but at a moderating pace, easing from 2.9% growth early in 2026 to 1.6% by year-end. It is showing deceleration in growth, showing demand is resilient but losing momentum.

New residential construction, on the other hand, appears to be mixed, according to Census. While total building permits and housing starts are trending below prior-year levels, single-family housing starts and completions showed some sequential improvement. Overall, new construction still showed weakness on a year-over-year basis despite some short-term improvement in single-family activity. Combined with new construction indicators, it implies that overall housing-related demand remains stable, but it is not strong enough for a meaningful recovery. Future improvement would largely be contingent on interest rates easing.

Margins Under Pressure

The ongoing cost challenges continue to put pressure on FBIN’s profitability. Management is expecting profitability to soften in 2026 as tariffs, commodity inflation, and higher SG&A weigh on earnings. Notably, most tariff costs are borne domestically rather than by foreign exporters, which would highlight how tariffs will continue to function as a burden to manufacturers. A New York Fed study indicates that nearly 90% of the costs associated with the tariffs were absorbed by domestic businesses and consumers rather than foreign exporters. As a result, margin recovery will likely rely more on structural cost improvements than on near-term tailwinds. Despite the firm’s ability to offset tariff costs through pricing actions, margins were not entirely protected.

Operational Resilience and Portfolio Strength

Despite a tough backdrop, the firm is putting up some encouraging signs of operational resilience. Management has highlighted ongoing market share gains even as housing demand remained subdued. Noting that they have outperformed the market for their products by 130 basis points for the full year and 300 basis points for the fourth quarter. Their higher-end categories, the House of Rohl luxury portfolio, delivered solid growth, showing relative resilience in the premium segments. Its connected products, such as Flo and Yale smart locks, show encouraging momentum. These drivers don’t eliminate the macro headwinds, but they do provide stability as of now and set up upside if demand firms.

Relative Valuation Model

In my relative valuation model above, I will be comparing FBIN’s performance against a selected group of peers. The key metrics that I have used for comparison include growth outlook and trailing twelve months [TTM] profitability margins. I will be using forward revenue growth as a forward-looking indicator, while profitability is evaluated using EBITDA margin TTM and net income margin TTM.

From a growth perspective, FBIN’s forward revenue growth of 0.69%, lags most peers. Although it is broadly in line with Masco's (MAS) growth profile, it trails companies such as Allegion (ALLE), Resideo (REZI), and A.O. Smith (AOS), which are expected to post stronger revenue growth.

Turning to profitability, its EBITDA margin TTM of 19.05% remains competitive relative to peers, broadly comparable to MAS and AOS. However, its net income margin TTM of 6.69% is lower than most of its peers.

FBIN’s forward P/E of 14.60x sits below the peer group median. Its discounted valuation is broadly consistent with its slower growth and weaker net profitability. That said, the gap looks more pronounced given the company’s competitive EBITDA margin profile. With demand trends still uncertain and cost headwinds ongoing, maintaining the current multiple remains a prudent assumption.

Applying my target P/E multiple of 14.60x to its 2027 estimate, my 2027 target price is $59.70. Representing a potential upside of roughly 16%. While weaker growth and near-term margin pressure justify some discount, the gap may appear notable if profitability stabilizes or housing demand improves.

Conclusion

In my earlier coverage, I have given it a Hold rating. The company’s market share gains, brand portfolio strength, and earnings recovery potential support a more positive outlook even though its growth remains modest and margins are subject to ongoing pressures. While near-term headwinds persist, FBIN’s valuation and potential recovery support a Buy stance.

data.title: Diamond Hill Long-Short Strategy Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Chris Bingaman, CFA

Portfolio Manager

Nate Palmer, CFA, CPA

Portfolio Manager

Brian Fontanella, CFA

Portfolio Specialist

Market and portfolio review

Equity markets continued to move higher in Q4, with the Russell 1000 Index increasing 2.41% to end the year up 17.37%. From a sector perspective, health care (+11.2%) was the top-performing sector this quarter, as it seemed to garner renewed investor attention given depressed valuations and several quarters of increased uncertainty. Communication services (+6.1%) was the next-best-performing sector, solely driven by the performance of Alphabet (+29%), which composes approximately 50% of the sector. Sectors that declined tended to lean defensive, including real estate (-2.8%), utilities (-1.5%) and consumer staples (-0.4%).

The strategy outperformed the long-only benchmark in Q4, helped by nearly 9 percentage points of positive long–short spread (the performance of our long positions relative to the performance of our short positions), with both the long and short portfolios adding alpha relative to the Russell 1000 Index. It was a similar story for the full year, with more than 20 percentage points of positive long-short spread, and positive long and short book alpha driving the Fund’s outperformance.

While we continue to find compelling long opportunities, it has been a particularly fruitful environment for new short opportunities, as elevated expectations for a range of stocks have pushed valuations higher. In many situations, those elevated expectations have proven unwarranted, which has helped drive significant alpha among our consumer-oriented short positions, in particular.

Net exposure of 57% remains modestly below our 60% long-term average, with long exposure ending the year around 88% and short exposure around 31%. This leaves gross exposure around 120%, a level we would like to modestly increase over time. However, we will be disciplined in doing so.

Key contributors

Long portfolio

  • Shares of media and technology company Alphabet (GOOGL) were strong in Q4 on better-than-expected artificial intelligence (AI)-driven growth, particularly in its Google Search, Google Cloud and YouTube businesses.
  • Ciena (CIEN), a networking systems company, saw shares increase meaningfully amid optimism around hyperscaler and AI-related demand. The company has also benefited from growing cloud spending, which has supported its pluggables and data interconnect business.
  • Citigroup (C), a diversified global bank, outperformed in Q4 as strong Q3 results showed continued improvement in returns, driven by disciplined expense control and accelerating capital return.

Short portfolio

All key contributors were long positions.

Key detractors

Long portfolio

  • Perrigo (PRGO), an over-the-counter (OTC) pharmaceuticals manufacturer, saw shares decline following weak results in its infant formula and European OTC businesses. Long term, the company is conducting a strategic review of its infant formula business and will refocus on consumer-oriented operations by expanding the range of products offered alongside its store-brand OTC portfolio, actions which we view positively.
  • Social media platform Meta Platforms (META) underperformed during the quarter due to a sharp increase in AI-related spending that was viewed unfavorably by the market.
  • Shares of software and technology provider Microsoft (MSFT) declined as investors became more cautious about funding for large AI investments being made by customers, including OpenAI.

Short portfolio

All key contributors were long positions.

New positions

Long Portfolio

  • SentinelOne (S) is a key player in the cybersecurity space, with a strong position in endpoint security software which it has leveraged to expand into cloud data/AI security. After booming during COVID and work-from-home, sentiment is now that the growth in the number of endpoints needing security will continue to slow. While the market will not grow at the rates it had been, the market should still grow high single digits, and we believe the company will continue to take share due to its best-in-class technology.
  • Diversified industrial conglomerate Dover (DOV) is in the middle of an underappreciated transition from a merger-and-acquisition–focused holding company to a more execution-focused operating company, which we believe creates a long runway for continued margin expansion. Furthermore, recent portfolio moves have increased its exposure to higher-growth end markets, and after several years of sluggish growth, various cyclical headwinds seem likely to subside. We initiated a position with the stock trading at a significant discount to our estimate of its intrinsic value.

Short portfolio

  • School bus and parts manufacturer Blue Bird (BLBD) has seen a strong increase in profits since 2019, driven by outsized profitability on its electric school buses. However, we believe the company’s profitability will be pressured moving forward due to the Trump administration’s halting of federal funding for electric school buses, recent expirations for electric vehicle tax credits and structural headwinds in public school funding.
  • We initiated a short position in Pure Storage (PSTG), a provider of enterprise data storage systems and software, as we believe its recent share gains will be constrained by competitive dynamics within the storage market moving forward. In addition, we view the company’s opportunity to capture hyperscaler and AI–related storage demand as limited, which tempers our longer-term growth outlook.
  • Host Hotels & Resorts (HST), the largest US hotel REIT, operates in an industry where benefits of scale tend to favor franchise brands over asset-heavy owners. The lodging business is also inherently exposed to labor and utility costs and carries high capital requirements. While Host’s higher-end consumer is faring well currently, an economic downturn would likely pressure results as we believe franchise brands would outperform in that environment.
  • US-focused crypto broker and platform Coinbase Global (COIN) has faced both slowing momentum in its core consumer trading business and increased competition, and the company is also pivoting into more competitive areas, including equity trading and predictive markets. We believe the market is overlooking these structural and cyclical headwinds, so we initiated a short position in Q4.

Eliminated positions

Long portfolio

  • Business process management company WNS Holdings was eliminated from the portfolio after its acquisition by Capgemini closed in an all-cash transaction.
  • Shares of apparel retailer lululemon (LULU), medical supply manufacturer Teleflex (TFX), and media and technology company Comcast (CMCSA) were sold to fund more attractive investment opportunities as the fundamentals for each were weaker than we had expected.

Short portfolio

All eliminated positions were long positions.
